parents.father_research_notes: Of Northampton MA. Elisha Sr. left his son Elisha Jr. the family homestead; Elisha Jr. exchanged it with his brother Theodore and left Northampton.
parents.mother_research_notes: Of Northampton MA, maiden name Janes.

burial.plot_notes: Cimetière Mont-Royal, Outremont Montréal — per FA #251558309. Family memorial commemorates Elisha together with all three of his wives.

career.notes: Per EH bio: farmer who settled successively at Conway MA, Derby VT, Montreal (where he resided from 1815 to 1828, thirteen years), Sunderland MA, and Champlain NY. Inherited the family homestead from his father Elisha Sr. but exchanged it with his brother Theodore and left Northampton.

narrative_blocks.header: Six-state migration across forty years, three wives
narrative_blocks.body: Elisha Lyman Jr. married three times — Hannah Stiles in 1793 (died 1814), Thankful Hunt the same year Hannah died, and Sarah Beaumont in 1837 the same year Thankful died. He moved his family across an extraordinary geographic arc: Northampton Massachusetts to Conway Massachusetts, then Derby Vermont, then thirteen years in Montreal Canada from 1815 to 1828, then Sunderland Massachusetts, finally Champlain New York where he died in 1844 at age seventy-four.

narrative_blocks.header: Memorialized with all three wives in Montreal
narrative_blocks.body: Elisha Lyman Jr. died at Champlain New York February 21, 1844 at age seventy-four. He was buried at Cimetière Mont-Royal in Montreal — the city where his second wife Thankful had died in 1837, and where his sons William, Benjamin, and Henry had settled to build the Lyman Brothers drug empire. A family memorial at Mount Royal commemorates Elisha together with all three of his wives.

parents.father_research_notes: Of Northampton MA. Elisha Sr. left his son Elisha Jr. the family homestead; Elisha Jr. exchanged it with his brother Theodore and left Northampton.
parents.mother_research_notes: Of Northampton MA, maiden name Janes.

burial.plot_notes: Cimetière Mont-Royal, Outremont Montréal — per FA #251558309. Family memorial commemorates Elisha together with all three of his wives.

career.notes: Per EH bio: farmer who settled successively at Conway MA, Derby VT, Montreal (where he resided from 1815 to 1828, thirteen years), Sunderland MA, and Champlain NY. Inherited the family homestead from his father Elisha Sr. but exchanged it with his brother Theodore and left Northampton.

narrative_blocks.header: Six-state migration across forty years, three wives
narrative_blocks.body: Elisha Lyman Jr. married three times — Hannah Stiles in 1793 (died 1814), Thankful Hunt the same year Hannah died, and Sarah Beaumont in 1837 the same year Thankful died. He moved his family across an extraordinary geographic arc: Northampton Massachusetts to Conway Massachusetts, then Derby Vermont, then thirteen years in Montreal Canada from 1815 to 1828, then Sunderland Massachusetts, finally Champlain New York where he died in 1844 at age seventy-four.

narrative_blocks.header: Memorialized with all three wives in Montreal
narrative_blocks.body: Elisha Lyman Jr. died at Champlain New York February 21, 1844 at age seventy-four. He was buried at Cimetière Mont-Royal in Montreal — the city where his second wife Thankful had died in 1837, and where his sons William, Benjamin, and Henry had settled to build the Lyman Brothers drug empire. A family memorial at Mount Royal commemorates Elisha together with all three of his wives.

burial.plot_notes: Derby Center Cemetery, Orleans County Vermont — per FindAGrave #235170328. Died a year before husband Elisha relocated the surviving family to Montreal.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at forty-five, before family's Montreal move
narrative_blocks.body: Hannah Stiles Lyman died February 25, 1814 at Derby Vermont, age forty-five — one year before her widower Elisha relocated the surviving family to Montreal where they would remain for thirteen years. She had borne ten children over twenty years; two had died in infancy (Ashbel Stiles in 1796 and Horace in 1798) and the youngest, Henry, was only four months old when she died. Her three eldest surviving sons would become the founders of Lyman Brothers, the largest pharmaceutical wholesaler in Canada.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of the Lyman drug dynasty
narrative_blocks.body: Three of Hannah Stiles Lyman's sons — William (Montreal druggist), Benjamin (co-founder of Lyman Brothers, the largest pharmaceutical company in Canada), and Henry (Montreal lawyer and Lyman Brothers partner) — together built the Lyman drug empire across Montreal and Toronto. Her grandson Henry Herbert Lyman would later bequeath his entomology collection to McGill University, founding what became the Lyman Entomological Museum.

burial.plot_notes: Mount Royal Cemetery Montreal — Montreal druggist, eldest son.

narrative_blocks.header: Eldest son, Montreal druggist before the Lyman dynasty
narrative_blocks.body: William Lyman was the eldest of Hannah Stiles and Elisha Lyman's ten children, born April 9, 1794 at Conway Massachusetts. He moved with the family to Montreal in 1815 at age twenty-one and established himself as a Montreal druggist — the first of his brothers to enter the pharmaceutical trade in Canada. He died at Champlain New York September 7, 1857 and was buried at Mount Royal Cemetery Montreal.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for maternal grandfather Ashbel Stiles of Windsor CT — the first sibling to bear both his grandfather's first and surname.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived seven days, namesake of grandfather
narrative_blocks.body: Ashbel Stiles Lyman was born May 27, 1796 at Conway Massachusetts and died June 3, 1796 — lived seven days. He was the second of Hannah Stiles and Elisha Lyman's children, named for his maternal grandfather Ashbel Stiles of Windsor Connecticut. His death came two years before another infant brother, Horace, died at Derby Vermont in April 1798.

narrative_blocks.header: First child born in Derby, lived weeks
narrative_blocks.body: Horace Lyman was born April 11, 1798 at Derby Vermont — the first of Hannah and Elisha's children born after the family's move from Conway Massachusetts. He died in April 1798, only weeks old. He was the second infant death for Hannah and Elisha; their previous son Ashbel Stiles had died at Conway in 1796.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Montreal mayor who died caring for immigrants
narrative_blocks.body: Hannah Lyman married John Easton Mills January 14, 1826 in Montreal. Her husband became Mayor of Montreal and died in 1847 of typhus contracted while personally caring for Irish famine immigrants in the city's fever sheds. She survived him by thirty-four years, eventually dying at Norwood near London England at age eighty-one in 1881.

narrative_blocks.header: Three sons all died in single 1841 year
narrative_blocks.body: Hannah Lyman Mills lost three of her sons in 1841: George at age six, Edwin at age two, and Albert in infancy. The pattern suggests a single epidemic or catastrophic event swept through the Mills household that year. She would lose her husband John Easton Mills to typhus six years later, leaving her with three daughters who all married into the Montreal mercantile elite.

notable.notable_blurb: Montreal mayor died of typhus tending Irish immigrants

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of John Easton Mills (1796-1847), Mayor of Montreal.

burial.plot_notes: Cimetière Mont-Royal Montreal — Plot Section G2, Number G204.

career.notes: Elected December 1846 after a deadlocked March 1846 council vote (Mills 10, incumbent James Ferrier 9 — Ferrier voted for himself twice per existing rules; municipal paralysis ensued until December 1846 when Mills was elected decisively). Less than one year in office before his death from typhus November 12, 1847.
Prominent Montreal merchant prior to election as mayor.

narrative_blocks.header: Mayor caught typhus tending Irish famine immigrants
narrative_blocks.body: John Easton Mills was elected Mayor of Montreal in December 1846 after months of deadlock on the city council. Less than a year into his term, the 1847 typhus outbreak struck Montreal among Irish famine immigrants. He personally organized containment measures and volunteered to tend the sick in the Pointe-Saint-Charles immigrant fever sheds — contracted typhus himself — and died November 12, 1847 after less than one year in office.

narrative_blocks.header: Three of his sons died in 1841
narrative_blocks.body: George at six, Edwin at two, and the infant Albert all died in 1841 — likely an epidemic year inside the household. His daughter Ada would die six decades later at the center of Montreal’s Redpath Mansion Mystery.

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Hannah Jane Mills Whitney (1826-1905).

notable.notable_blurb: Died with son in famous Redpath Mansion shooting

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law; Founded Canada's first sugar refinery 1854

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Ada Maria Mills Redpath (1842-1901).

burial.plot_notes: Cimetière Mont-Royal Montreal — Redpath family grave; buried Jun 15, 1901 with her son Jocelyn Clifford who died alongside her.

narrative_blocks.header: Marriage contract gave her control of her assets
narrative_blocks.body: Ada married John James Redpath in 1867 at Putney, England — an unusual cross-Atlantic ceremony for the Mills and Redpath families. Her marriage contract stipulated she would control her own assets as if no marriage had taken place — a striking provision for 1867. In November 1870 she purchased the family villa at 1065 Sherbrooke Street Montreal, which would become the site of her death thirty-one years later.

narrative_blocks.header: Died with son in famous Redpath Mansion shooting
narrative_blocks.body: On June 13, 1901 at six in the evening at the Redpath Mansion 1065 Sherbrooke Street Montreal, two revolver shots were heard from Ada's bedroom. Servants found her dead and her twenty-four-year-old son Jocelyn Clifford fatally wounded. The case attracted wide publicity given the Redpaths' Canadian Sugar Refining fortune, but the coroner's inquest was perfunctory; the prevailing theory held Ada had suicidal intent and Clifford was accidentally shot trying to take the revolver from her.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name Stiles carries the maternal great-grandfather's surname — Stiles naming pattern reaches its fifth Lyman/Mills generation descendant after Elisha Stiles Lyman, Edwin Stiles Lyman, Frederick Stiles Lyman, and Ashbel Stiles Lyman.

burial.plot_notes: Mount Royal Cemetery Montreal — died young at 24.

narrative_blocks.header: Stiles middle name, married Redpath brother
narrative_blocks.body: Alice Stiles Mills married George Drummond Redpath at Shanklin, Hampshire England on May 29, 1867 — the same year her older sister Ada Maria Mills married George's brother John James Redpath at Putney England. Two Mills sisters married two Redpath brothers within months, cementing a dual alliance between the families. Alice died two years later in 1869 at age twenty-four, while her sister lived another thirty-two years before her own death at the Redpath Mansion in 1901.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name Stiles carries the maternal grandfather's surname — second of three Lyman siblings (with Edwin Stiles and Frederick Stiles in the next generation) to bear it.

narrative_blocks.header: Died unmarried in Illinois at forty-eight
narrative_blocks.body: Elisha Stiles Lyman migrated from Vermont and Quebec to Ottawa Illinois at some point in the late 1840s — the only one of Hannah and Elisha's ten children to settle in the American West. He died unmarried at Ottawa Illinois March 12, 1852 at age forty-eight. His middle name Stiles carried the maternal grandfather's surname, a pattern repeated in his younger brother Edwin Stiles.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name Stiles carries the maternal grandfather's surname — sibling pattern with Elisha Stiles Lyman before him and Frederick Stiles Lyman in the next generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Died of cholera Montreal 1832, age twenty-six
narrative_blocks.body: Edwin Stiles Lyman died of cholera at Montreal September 22, 1832 at age twenty-six — during the first major North American cholera pandemic, which killed roughly four thousand Montrealers, or six percent of the city's population, in a single summer. He was unmarried. His death came seventeen years after the Lyman family had settled in Montreal in 1815 and four years after their 1828 departure for Sunderland Massachusetts — Edwin may have remained in Montreal when his father moved south.

HD0582.prominent_name_reason: Benjamin Lyman (1810-1878) — Thomas Hooker's great-great-great-grandson, Canadian chemist and drug manufacturer. Co-founded Lyman Bros & Co, the largest pharmaceutical company in Canada. Captain, 5th Company Montreal Rifles during the Lower Canada Rebellion of 1838.

notable.notable_blurb: Co-founded Lyman Brothers, largest Canadian pharmaceutical wholesaler

narrative_blocks.header: Built largest pharmaceutical company in Canada
narrative_blocks.body: Benjamin Lyman was born June 11, 1810 at Derby Vermont, the ninth of Hannah Stiles and Elisha Lyman's ten children. He co-founded Lyman Brothers and Company — the largest pharmaceutical wholesaler in Canada — with his older brother William and younger brother Henry. He served as Captain of the Fifth Company Montreal Militia and died at Toronto December 5, 1878.

narrative_blocks.header: A rifle captain in the Rebellion
narrative_blocks.body: Before the drug-wholesaling fortune, Lyman captained the 5th Company of the Montreal Rifles through the Lower Canada Rebellion of 1838. He lies at Mount Royal.

notable.notable_blurb: Quebec lawyer, Lyman Brothers Montreal drug merchant

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Henry Lyman (1813-1897), Montreal lawyer and Lyman Brothers partner.

military_service.notes: Some military service in Montreal per EH bio — specific war and dates undocumented.

burial.plot_notes: Cimetière Mont-Royal Montreal — buried with wife Mary Corse Lyman.

education.notes: Graduated McGill College, Montreal — per EH bio. McGill founded 1821 in Montreal; Henry would have attended in the 1830s.
Further study at a college in England per EH bio — specific institution undocumented.

education.school_name: Bar of the Province of Quebec
education.notes: Admitted to practice law in the Province of Quebec.

career.notes: Joined the family drug firm in 1829 at age 15-16. Partner in Lyman Brothers — the largest pharmaceutical wholesaler in Canada — alongside his older brothers William and Benjamin. Listed as Drug Merchant in St Antoine Ward Montreal in the 1881 census.
Admitted to practice law in the Province of Quebec; partner in Lyman, Class & Co. law firm. Distinct from Lyman Brothers drug firm.
Served on Montreal City Council as Superintendent of West Ward.

narrative_blocks.header: McGill graduate, Quebec lawyer, Lyman Brothers partner
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Lyman graduated McGill College Montreal and studied further in England before being admitted to practice law in the Province of Quebec. He joined the family drug firm in 1829 at age fifteen and became a partner in Lyman Brothers — the largest pharmaceutical wholesaler in Canada — while also serving on Montreal City Council as Superintendent of West Ward and as Deacon in the Congregational Church.

narrative_blocks.header: Three sons died young, four to old age
narrative_blocks.body: Henry and Mary Corse Lyman had seven children together in Montreal between 1844 and 1861. Three died young: Arthur Ellis as an infant in 1847, Susan Alice at age five in 1853, and Roswell Corse at forty-two in 1892. The four surviving sons lived long lives, including Henry Herbert (who perished on the Empress of Ireland 1914) and Walter Ernest who reached eighty-nine.

parents.father_research_notes: Surname Corse; family of Northfield Franklin Co MA. Not built per Jalapeño Rule.

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Mary Corse Lyman (1823-1908), wife of Henry Lyman.

burial.plot_notes: Cimetière Mont-Royal Montreal — buried with husband Henry Lyman.
Mount Royal Cemetery Montreal — per FA #108930165.

marriages.spouse_research_notes: Wife Louisa — maiden name not documented in FA or WikiTree. Listed as 'Louisa Lyman' which is ambiguous (could be married Lyman name or unusual case where surname matched). Could possibly be a Lyman cousin marriage; flagged for further research.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name Stiles carries the Stiles family name from his grandmother Hannah Stiles Lyman — third Lyman descendant to bear the Stiles middle (after great-uncle Elisha Stiles Lyman and Edwin Stiles Lyman in the previous generation).

narrative_blocks.header: Stiles middle name preserved into third generation
narrative_blocks.body: Frederick Stiles Lyman was the eldest of Henry and Mary Corse Lyman's seven children, born January 6, 1844 in Montreal. His middle name Stiles preserved the Hooker-Stiles maternal lineage into a third Canadian-born generation — following the pattern set by his uncles Elisha Stiles and Edwin Stiles Lyman. He married Louisa Lyman, had a daughter Edith Mary Louise, and died at sixty-four in Montreal in 1908, buried at Mount Royal Cemetery.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name Corse carries his mother's maiden surname.

HD0587.prominent_name_reason: Henry Herbert Lyman (1854-1914) — entomologist, drug company heir. Leading authority on North American Lepidoptera; published 60+ papers. Bequeathed lepidoptera collection (~20,000 specimens) and $40,000 to McGill University at his death — founding the Lyman Entomological Museum at McGill. Perished with his wife on the Empress of Ireland, May 29, 1914.

notable.notable_blurb: McGill entomology bequest, perished Empress of Ireland 1914

education.notes: Early education at West End Academy, Montreal.
BA McGill University 1876 with the Logan medal in geology and natural science.

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Henry Herbert Lyman (1854-1914) from the Annual Report of the Fruit Growers' Association of Ontario, 1899-1900.

military_service.unit: 5th Battalion (5th Royal Highlanders), Canadian Voluntary Force
military_service.notes: Served 1877-1891, retired as Major. Peacetime service.

burial.plot_notes: Cenotaph at Cimetière Mont-Royal Montreal — body not recovered from the St. Lawrence River. Same cemetery as wife Florence's cenotaph.

career.notes: Joined the pharmacist company Lymans, Clare & Co. (Canada's largest pharmaceutical company, owned by his father Henry Lyman) after his 1880 MA. Succeeded his father as senior partner and as president of Lymans Limited at Toronto.

career.organization: 5th Battalion, Canadian Voluntary Force (5th Royal Highlanders)
career.notes: Served in the 5th Battalion of the Canadian Voluntary Force from 1877; retired as Major in 1891. Also referred to as 5th Royal Highlanders.

Supported the British Empire; was an organizer of the Imperial Federation League of Canada — late-19th-century movement advocating closer political and economic union between Britain and her self-governing colonies.

Member since 1875. Vice president of Montreal branch 1877. President 1881 and again 1888-1899. Published over 60 papers, mainly in The Canadian Entomologist. Collected butterflies and moths primarily on the Atlantic Coast on summer holidays; took interest in rearing them. Bequest of ~20,000 specimens + entomology books + $40,000 to McGill University founded what became the Lyman Entomological Museum and Research Laboratory (moved to Quebec 1961).

narrative_blocks.header: McGill Logan medal, Lyman Entomological Museum bequest
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Herbert Lyman graduated McGill University in 1876 with the Logan medal in geology and natural science, and added an MA in 1880. As Canada's leading authority on North American Lepidoptera he published over sixty papers, mainly in The Canadian Entomologist. His 1914 bequest to McGill University — approximately twenty thousand insect specimens, his entomology library, and forty thousand dollars — founded what became the Lyman Entomological Museum and Research Laboratory.

narrative_blocks.header: Drowned on delayed honeymoon, Empress of Ireland
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Herbert Lyman cared for his ailing mother until her death and married Florence Holwell Kirkby in March 1912 at age fifty-seven. The couple finally found time to travel two years later — boarding the Empress of Ireland on May 28, 1914 for their delayed honeymoon to Europe. Early on May 29, 1914 the ship collided in the St. Lawrence River and sank in fourteen minutes; both Lymans were among the 840 passengers and 172 crew lost.

career.notes: Practiced as a notary public in Montreal.

narrative_blocks.header: Montreal notary public, lived eighty-two years
narrative_blocks.body: Albert Clarence Lyman was born in Montreal in 1856, the sixth of Henry and Mary Corse Lyman's seven children. He practiced as a notary public in Montreal — a Quebec civil-law legal profession distinct from his father Henry's role as advocate in the Lyman Class law firm. He lived to age eighty-two, dying in 1938 and buried at Mount Royal Cemetery with his parents.

narrative_blocks.header: First documented within-tree cousin marriage 1882
narrative_blocks.body: Albert Clarence Lyman married Florence Anna Whitney on June 28, 1882 in Montreal — the first within-tree cousin marriage documented in the Hooker descendants project. Albert and Florence were both Hooker descendants through different children of Hannah Stiles Lyman: Albert's father Henry Lyman and Florence's grandmother Hannah Lyman Mills were siblings. Their three children carry two independent paths of descent from Thomas Hooker.

burialCemetery.name: Cimetière Mont-Royal
burialCemetery.notes: Cimetière Mont-Royal (Mount Royal Cemetery), Outremont, Montreal QC. Major Protestant cemetery founded 1852 on the slopes of Mount Royal. Burial site of multiple members of the Lyman family — Hannah Stiles Lyman's descendants who became prominent Montreal druggists, lawyers, and civic figures: Elisha Lyman Jr. (with his three wives memorialized together), Henry Lyman, Mary Corse Lyman, Henry Herbert Lyman (later commemorated here after death on the Empress of Ireland 1914), Albert Clarence Lyman, Frederick Stiles Lyman, Edith Mary Louise Lyman, and others.
